back_translation: After these events, Jesus crossed over the Sea of Galilee (that is, the Sea of Tiberias) to the other side.
A large crowd followed Him because they had seen the signs that He performed on the sick.
Jesus then went up on the mountain and stayed there with His disciples.
At that time the Passover Festival, which is a festival of the Jews, was drawing near.
When Jesus lifted up His eyes and saw a large crowd coming toward Him, He said to Philip, "Where shall we buy bread for these people to eat?"
He said this to test Philip, for He already knew what He was going to do.
Philip answered Him, "Bread of two hundred denarii would not be enough for everyone to have even a little bit each."
Andrew, the younger brother of Simon Peter, who was one of His disciples, said to Him,
"Here is a boy who has five barley loaves and two small fish. But how can these feed so many people?"
Jesus said, "Have all the people sit down." There was much grass there, so the men sat down, numbering about five thousand.
Then Jesus took the bread, gave thanks, and distributed to those who were seated; and He likewise distributed the small fish to them as much as they wanted.
When everyone was full, He said to His disciples, "Gather up the remaining pieces; do not let even a little be lost."
So they collected and got pieces from the five barley loaves left over from those who had eaten, filling twelve baskets full.
When the people saw the sign He had performed, they said, "Truly this man is the prophet who was promised to come into the world."
Jesus knew that those people were about to come and force Him to be made king, so He withdrew up the mountain alone again.
When evening came, His disciples went down to the sea,
and got into a boat heading across the sea to the city of Capernaum. By that time it was dark, and Jesus had not yet come to them.
And the sea was being stirred up because the wind was blowing strongly.
When they had rowed about twenty-five or thirty stadia, they saw Jesus walking on the sea coming near the boat, and they were terrified.
He said to them, "It is I myself; do not be afraid."
They were then willing to receive Him into the boat, and at that very moment the boat reached the shore where they were heading.

On the next day, the crowd that still remained on the other side of the sea saw that there had been only one small boat there, and saw that Jesus had not gone aboard with His disciples, but the disciples had gone alone.
But several small boats came from the city of Tiberias, arriving near the place where the people had eaten the bread after the Lord had given thanks.
So when the crowd saw that neither Jesus nor the disciples were there, they got into those small boats and went to the city of Capernaum to look for Jesus.
When they found Him on the other side of the sea, they asked, "Rabbi, when did You come here?"
Jesus answered them, "Amen, amen, I say to you all, you are looking for Me not because you saw signs but because you ate bread until you were full."
"Do not work for food that perishes, but work for food that endures to eternal life, which the Son of Man will give to you. For God who is the Father has set His seal of approval on Him."
Then they asked Him, "What must we do in order to do the works of God?"
Jesus answered them, "The work of God is this — that you believe in the One whom He has sent."
They then asked Him, "What sign then will You perform for us to see and believe You? What are You doing?"
Our forefathers ate the manna in the wilderness, as it is written, 'He gave them bread from heaven to eat.'"
So Jesus said to them, "Amen, amen, I say to you, it was not Moses who gave the bread from heaven to you, but it is My Father who is giving you the true bread from heaven.
For the bread of God is the One who comes down from heaven and gives life to the world."
Then they said to Him, "Sir, give us this bread at all times."
Jesus said to them, "I am the bread of life. The one who comes to Me will not hunger at all, and the one who believes in Me will not thirst again.
But as I have already told you, even though you have seen Me, you still do not believe.
Everyone whom the Father gives to Me will come to Me, and the one who comes to Me, I will never cast out.
For I came down from heaven not to do My own will, but to do the will of the One who sent Me.
And the will of the One who sent Me is this — that I will not lose anyone whom He has given Me, even one, but will raise them up on the last day.
For the will of My Father is this — that everyone who looks upon the Son and believes in Him may have eternal life, and I will raise that one up on the last day."
The Jewish leaders then began to grumble about Him because He had said, "I am the bread that came down from heaven."
They said, "Is this not Jesus, the son of Joseph, whose father and mother we know? Why does he now say, 'I came down from heaven'?"
Jesus answered them, "Stop grumbling among yourselves.
No one can come to Me unless the Father who sent Me draws him, and I will raise that one up on the last day.
It is written in the book of the Prophets, 'Everyone will be taught by God.' Everyone who has listened to the Father and has learned, comes to Me.
Not that anyone has seen the Father, except the One who comes from God; this One has seen the Father.
Amen, amen, I say to you, the one who believes has eternal life.
I am the bread of life.
Your forefathers ate the manna in the wilderness, but they still died.
This is the bread that came down from heaven, so that whoever eats this bread will not die.
I am the living bread that came down from heaven. If anyone eats this bread, that person will live forever, and the bread that I will give is My flesh, given for the life of the world."
The Jewish leaders then began to argue among themselves, "How can this man give His flesh for us to eat?"
Jesus then said to them, "Amen, amen, I say to you, if you do not eat the flesh of the Son of Man and do not drink His blood, you have no life in yourselves.
The one who eats My flesh and drinks My blood has eternal life, and I will raise that one up on the last day.
For My flesh is true food, and My blood is true drink.
The one who eats My flesh and drinks My blood abides in Me, and I am in him.
Just as the living Father sent Me and I live because of the Father, the one who eats Me will also live because of Me.
This is the bread that came down from heaven, not like that which the forefathers ate and still died. The one who eats this bread will live forever."
He said these things while teaching in the synagogue at the city of Capernaum.
When His disciples heard these teachings, many of them said, "This teaching is exceedingly hard to accept. Who can listen to it?"
Jesus knew within Himself that His disciples were grumbling about this, so He said to them, "Does this make you stumble?
If you were to see the Son of Man ascending up to the place where He used to be, then?
The Spirit is the One who gives life; as for the flesh, it has no benefit at all. The words I have spoken to you are Spirit and are life.
But there are some among you who do not believe." (For Jesus knew from the beginning who would be the ones not believing, and who would betray Him.)
He continued saying, "For this reason I have told you that no one can come to Me unless the Father graciously grants it to him."
From that time on, many of His disciples turned back to their former way and no longer followed Him.
Jesus then said to the twelve disciples, "Do you also want to leave?"
Simon Peter answered Him, "Lord, to whom shall we go? You have the words of eternal life,
and we have believed and have come to know that You are the Holy One of God."
Jesus answered them, "Did I not Myself choose you twelve? Yet one of you is a devil."
He spoke of Judas, son of Simon of Iscariot, for although Judas was one of the twelve disciples, he would betray Him.
